did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper includes multiple studies using AI/ML/DL for dermatology tasks: 1) Szczekulski et al. used a ResNet-50 Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with transfer learning to predict the efficacy rate of treating Port-Wine Stain birthmarks from 2D photographs. 2) Jiang et al. used deep-learning deblurring models (transformer architecture) and CNN-based diagnostic models to restore accuracy of blurry teledermatology images. 3) Duverdier et al. used Bayesian machine learning models (EczemaPred) to predict patient-reported atopic dermatitis severity scores (POEM). 4) Bishnoi et al. used deep neural networks and ensemble neural networks for wound assessment and outcome prediction.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document contains abstracts (210-215) from the Journal of Investigative Dermatology (2023) Volume 143, which present original experimental studies. Specifically, it includes research on mouse scratching detection using deep learning (Peng et al.), predicting Port-Wine Stain treatment efficacy using CNNs (Szczekulski et al.), deblurring teledermatology images (Jiang et al.), skin cancer classification using transfer learning (Chen & Lai), wound assessment using multi-modal data (Bishnoi et al.), and predicting atopic dermatitis severity using Bayesian machine learning (Duverdier et al.). These are original technical and clinical studies, not reviews.

title: Investigating the effectiveness of convolutional neural networks in predicting the efficacy rate of treating port-wine stain birthmark
brief_summary: This study investigated the effectiveness of convolutional neural networks (CNNs) in predicting the treatment efficacy for Port-Wine Stain birthmarks. The researchers utilized a ResNet-50 CNN with transfer learning on 2D photographs taken prior to treatment from 44 Caucasian participants aged 8 to 59. The model aimed to predict the numerical improvement in clearance after patients underwent treatment with a 532nm laser. The AI consistently achieved greater than 70% accuracy when classifying outcomes into categories of improvement over or below 62%. The results demonstrate the potential of CNNs to predict treatment success, although the authors note that larger datasets may be needed for precise numerical predictions.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary subject of the study by Szczekulski et al. (Abstract 211), where a CNN was used to predict treatment efficacy for Port-Wine Stain birthmarks in 44 Caucasian participants.

all_task_types.details: Abstract 210 (Peng et al.) classifies video frames into 'scratch' and 'non-scratch' categories using Scaled-YOLOv4 for localization and a rule-based method for counting. Metrics include recall, precision, and F1-score for both classes.

Abstract 211 (Szczekulski et al.) uses a ResNet-50 CNN to classify treatment outcomes into two discrete categories: 'improvement over 62%' or 'lower than 62% improvement'. The model achieved >70% accuracy in this binary classification task. While the aim was to predict numerical improvement, the reported primary result is categorical classification.

all_task_types.target: Skin tumor types (Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Seborrheic Keratosis, Nevus, Other Skin Tumors, Normal Skin)
all_task_types.details: Abstract 213 (Chen & Lai) performs multi-class classification of skin tumors using CNNs (inception_v3, inception_resnet_v2, NASNET_large) to differentiate malignant from benign lesions. Metrics include prediction accuracy and sensitivity.

all_task_types.target: Wound bed tissue types (Granulation, Slough, Necrosis, Epithelialization)
all_task_types.details: Abstract 214 (Bishnoi et al.) uses a deep neural network to classify tissue types in the wound bed. It also performs binary classification for wound healing outcome prediction (healed vs. not healed) at various time points, reporting AUC metrics.

Abstract 211 (Szczekulski et al.) states the aim was to predict the 'numerical improvement (total clearance)' and notes that more data could deliver an AI for 'exact numerical prediction'. However, the reported results are for the categorical classification task.

Abstract 215 (Duverdier et al.) uses Bayesian machine learning (EczemaPred) to predict continuous POEM severity scores. The model predicts future scores based on past scores, with performance measured by the probability of the predicted score being within a certain range (e.g., 3 points) of the measured score.

datasets_used.name: Mouse Scratching Video Clips
datasets_used.description: Video clips collected for mouse scratching detection and counting analysis in mouse models.

use.training: Used for training the scratching detection and counting system (Scaled-YOLOv4 and rule-based method) with stratified 5-fold cross-validation.
use.validation: Used for validation via stratified 5-fold cross-validation.
use.testing: Used for testing the proposed system's recall, precision, and F1-score.

datasets_used.name: Port-Wine Stain Clinical Cohort
datasets_used.description: A cohort of 44 Caucasian participants aged 8 to 59 evaluated with 3D imaging before and after treatment with a 532nm laser with large spot and contact cooling. 2D photographs taken prior to treatment were used.

use.training: Used to train a ResNet-50 Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with transfer learning to predict numerical improvement (total clearance).
use.validation: Used for validation during model training (losses decreased steadily).
use.testing: Used to evaluate the AI's accuracy on previously unseen data (labelling into categories).

datasets_used.name: Dermnet Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset consisting of 19,191 images of 23 skin disease categories, used to generate simulated blurry images for training deblurring models.

use.training: Used to create simulated blurry images for training a deep-learning deblurring model with a transformer architecture.
use.other: Source of simulated data for deblurring model training.

datasets_used.name: Biopsy-Proven Skin Tumor Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset of biopsy-proven clinical images of skin tumors, evenly distributed as training and test sets. Includes melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, seborrheic keratosis, nevus, other skin tumors, and normal health controls.

use.training: Used to establish diagnostic tools based on three CNN deep learning modules (inception_v3, inception_resnet_v2, and NASNET_large).
use.testing: Used as the test set, evenly distributed with the training set.

datasets_used.name: Singapore’s First Chronic Wound Registry
datasets_used.description: A registry containing comprehensive clinical data from 820 patients with chronic wounds and more than 2,000 wound images collected longitudinally over a follow-up period of 6 months. Wound images were annotated by trained wound care nurses and physicians.

use.training: Used to train a deep neural network for tissue type classification and an ensemble neural network for wound healing outcome prediction.
use.other: Source of multi-modal data (clinical data and wound images) for developing machine learning algorithms.

datasets_used.name: Atopic Dermatitis Patient Cohort (POEM)
datasets_used.description: Data from 196 AD patients aged 11 to 65 with weekly POEM scores over 16 weeks.

use.training: Used to train the EczemaPred Bayesian machine learning models to predict the evolution of POEM symptoms.
use.other: Used to evaluate prediction performance (probability of predicted score being within 3 or 1 point of measured score).

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: probability of predicted score being within a certain range of measured score

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the proposed mouse scratching detection and counting system.
To compare sharpness ratings of blurry vs. deblurred images provided by dermatologists.
To learn model dynamics from past AD severity scores and predict future POEM scores in the EczemaPred model.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no difference in sharpness ratings between blurry and deblurred images.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: Deblurred images have higher sharpness ratings than blurry images.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: Dermatologists gave significantly higher sharpness ratings for deblurred images compared to blurry images (mean difference: 0.64, p<0.001).

Mouse Scratching Video Clips.notes: Evaluated using stratified 5-fold cross-validation on 566 video clips (40,502 frames). The system achieved real-time simultaneous recognition of multiple objects.

Port-Wine Stain Clinical Cohort.notes: The AI consistently performed with greater than 70% accuracy when labelling previously unseen data. Training and validation losses decreased steadily. The study notes that more data could potentially deliver an AI for exact numerical prediction.

Dermnet Dataset (Simulated Blurry Images.metric: Accuracy drop quantification, Sensitivity improvement, Precision improvement
Dermnet Dataset (Simulated Blurry Images.notes: The deblurring model restored performance of CNN models on blurry images. More severe performance drops were observed in conventional CNN models with increasingly blurry images.

Real-world Blurry Dermatology Images.notes: Dermatologists gave significantly higher sharpness ratings for deblurred images than blurry images.

Biopsy-Proven Skin Tumor Dataset.notes: Models demonstrated consistent and exceptional high accuracy in differentiating malignant tumors from benign lesions. Data was evenly distributed as training and test sets.

Singapore’s First Chronic Wound Registry.notes: Wound images were annotated by trained wound care nurses and physicians. Preliminary results show high accuracy in segmentation of wound bed area.

Atopic Dermatitis Patient Cohort (POEM).metric: Probability of predicted score being within range of measured score
Atopic Dermatitis Patient Cohort (POEM).notes: Predictions were based on 8 weekly POEM scores. Performance plateaued after using previous 2 months' data. Prediction did not improve significantly by using daily symptom scores. POEM is subject to large measurement errors due to imperfect recall.

architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning (specific pretraining dataset not explicitly named, but implied as standard ImageNet or similar given 'transfer learning' context in abstract 211; however, abstract 213 explicitly mentions using biopsy-proven clinical images for training from scratch or fine-tuning without specifying a pre-trained backbone like ImageNet for the skin tumor models, while abstract 211 just says 'transfer learning'. For the specific ResNet-50 in abstract 211, pretraining details are not explicitly detailed beyond 'transfer learning'.)

training_details.dataset: 2D photographs taken prior to treatment from 44 Caucasian participants aged 8 to 59
training_details.target: Numerical improvement (total clearance) of Port-Wine Stain birthmarks after 532nm laser treatment
training_details.evaluation_method: Stratified 5-fold cross-validation (implied by context of other studies in the same abstract collection, though abstract 211 specifically mentions 'labelling previously unseen data' and 'training and validation losses decreased steadily')

classification_or_output_head.details: Aimed to predict numerical improvement (total clearance). Reported results were for binary classification into categories: 'improvement over 62%' or 'lower than 62% improvement'.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Deep neural network (for wound tissue classification)
Ensemble neural network (for wound healing outcome prediction)
preprocessing_techniques: Simulated blurring of images from the Dermnet dataset to create training data for deblurring models
Deblurring of blurry images using deep-learning models with transformer architecture
Segmentation of cages in video clips for mouse scratching detection
Localization of mouse positions in video frames
Standardized annotation of wound images by trained wound care nurses and physicians
Aggregation of predicted symptom scores to obtain POEM scores

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Simulated blurry images were generated from the Dermnet dataset (19,191 images of 23 skin disease categories) to train deep-learning deblurring models with a transformer architecture.
Used for data splitting and evaluation in the mouse scratching detection study (Abstract 210) to evaluate the proposed system's recall, precision, and F1-score.
In the skin cancer classification study (Abstract 213), biopsy-proven clinical images of skin tumors were evenly distributed as training and test sets.

conclusions: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) combined with transfer learning can effectively predict the efficacy of Port-Wine Stain treatment, achieving greater than 70% accuracy in classifying outcomes as 'improvement over 62%' or 'lower than 62% improvement'.

The accuracy of the Port-Wine Stain prediction model may vary slightly due to the limited amount of data available; incorporating more data could potentially enable exact numerical prediction of treatment success.

Deep-learning deblurring models using a transformer architecture can restore the diagnostic accuracy of CNN-based models on blurry teledermatology images, with mean sensitivity improvements of 0.14 and 0.17 on slightly and heavily blurred images, respectively.

Deblurred images received significantly higher sharpness ratings from dermatologists compared to blurry images (mean difference: 0.64, p<0.001), indicating that deep learning models can increase perceived image sharpness.

CNN-based diagnostic modules (inception_v3, inception_resnet_v2, and NASNET_large) achieved high prediction accuracy (88.4%, 87.40%, and 88.01%, respectively) and sensitivity (87.26%, 86.49%, and 87.64%, respectively) in differentiating malignant tumors from benign lesions using biopsy-proven clinical images.

Automated AI-assisted diagnostic modules for skin cancer classification demonstrate high accuracy and practicality, potentially sparing patients from invasive biopsies when clear clinical photos are used.
A deep neural network achieved segmentation accuracy of more than 90% for wound bed area from surrounding skin in chronic wound assessment.
An ensemble neural network utilizing multi-modal data (clinical data and wound images) predicted wound healing outcomes with AUCs of 72.4% at 1 month, 72.3% at 3 months, and 70.0% at 6 months.

The EczemaPred Bayesian machine learning model predicted next week's Patient-Oriented Eczema Measure (POEM) scores with a 72% probability of being within 3 points and a 44% probability of being within 1 point of the measured score.
Prediction performance for POEM scores plateaued after using previous 2 months' data, and did not improve significantly by using daily symptom scores for training.
POEM scores are subject to large measurement errors due to the imperfect recall of symptoms, which contributes to the challenges in predicting atopic dermatitis severity.
important_remarks: The Port-Wine Stain efficacy prediction study (Abstract 211) is limited by a small sample size (n=44 Caucasian participants), which may cause accuracy variation and prevents exact numerical prediction; the model only achieved >70% accuracy for categorical outcomes.

The skin cancer classification study (Abstract 213) utilized biopsy-proven images that were 'evenly distributed' as training and test sets, which does not reflect real-world class imbalance and may overestimate performance compared to clinical practice.

The wound healing prediction model (Abstract 214) relies on data from Singapore’s First Chronic Wound Registry (n=820), limiting generalizability to other populations; performance metrics (AUC ~70-72%) are moderate.

The atopic dermatitis severity prediction (Abstract 215) is constrained by the inherent limitation of the POEM score, which is subject to 'large measurement errors due to the imperfect recall of symptoms,' making accurate prediction challenging.
The deblurring study (Abstract 212) used simulated blurry images from the Dermnet dataset for training, which may not fully capture the complexity of real-world teledermatology image degradation.

The mouse scratching detection system (Abstract 210) was evaluated on a specific experimental setup (4 cages per clip), limiting immediate assessment of scalability to other monitoring configurations.
